text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

This year the victors will earn a unique reward - a piece of French history

A century on its lastOlympics, the Games are back inParis. In 1924, winners took home a medal featuring a naked athlete helping their defeated rival to their feet. This year’s best competitors will leave with a unique reward – a piece of the Eiffel Tower itself.

It’s the first time a Games medal will feature elements from a historic monument. Hexagonal scraps of metal from the Tower – in homage to France’s six-sided shape – are embedded at the centre of every medal.

Unveiled today at the landmark itself, the medals are embossed with “Paris 2024”, the flame logo, and the Olympic rings or Paralympic Agitos emblem.

Each weighing about 18g, the iron pieces come from old girders replaced during the Tower’s constant renovation process. After removal they have been transported to a secret vault, stripped of paint, polished, varnished, and given a new lease of life as Olympic gongs.

They are designed by Paris jewellery house Chaumet. Six clasps attach the iron pieces to the medals’ centre, a nod to the 2.5 million rivets that keep the tower upright. Embedded grooves radiate outwards like rays of light, inspired by a tiara from Chaumet’s back catalogue.

As well as traditional emblems of the Greek goddess Nike and the historic Acropolis in Athens on the reverse side, the organisers have added a small Eiffel Tower, just in case wearers forget where their medals come from.

Fear not: the blue Chaumet box they come with will include a certificate from the Eiffel Tower Operating Company stating that the iron pieces are real monument offcuts.

The Paralympic medals feature “Paris 2024” written in Braille – another French invention – and notches on the edges to indicate the colour: one for gold, two for silver, three for bronze. The flipside is a view of the Eiffel Tower’s intricate patterns from underneath.

“Having a gold medal is already something incredible,” Joachim Roncin, the Games organising committee’s head of design, said. “But we wanted to add this French touch and we thought that the Eiffel Tower would be this cherry on the top. Having a piece of it is a piece of history.”

There are strict rules to medal design, on which the International Committee (IOC) get the final say. Medals must show Nike, goddess of victory; the IOC’s and Organising Committee’s emblems; the full name of the Games; and the name of the sport or discipline.

It wasn’t until Beijing 2008 that new materials were incorporated, when a jade ring was inlaid on the medals’ reverse side to blend Chinese culture and Greek antiquity together.

Silver medals have historically been made of real silver, but no Games since Stockholm 1912 have splashed out on solid gold for first prize.

Victors typically receive a gong made of gilt silver or silver with gold plating, as is the case in Paris this year, where each medal will include 6g of gold.

This year the gold medals weigh 529g, the silvers 525g, and the bronzes 455g.

The Paris Mint will produce 5,084 medals, roughly 2,600 for the Olympics and 2,400 for the Paralympics, out of fully recycled metal. Extras will go to museums and stored as spares in case medals need reassigning after the Games due to doping.

For example, last month the US figure skating team learned they would receive a very belated gold medal after the Court of Arbitration for Sport (CAS) suspended teenage prodigy Kamila Valieva, demoting the Russian Olympic Committee from first to fourth place in the team event.

Valieva is serving a four-year ban following the finding of trimetazidine in a December 2021 sample, though she has denied any wrongdoing.

Recent years have seen a shift in medal design to reflect environmental concerns. Recycling points were installed all over Japan in 2020 to collect used electronics for scraps, turning 78,985 tonnes of devices into 30.3kg of gold, 4,100kg of silver and 2,700kg of bronze.

At the 2016 Games, medals used gold extracted without using mercury, and, for silver gongs, old car parts and bits of mirror.

How much these sustainability measures offset the huge environmental impact of organising an Olympic Games is another question. However, that’s unlikely to be the first thing on the athletes’ minds as they celebrate with a piece of French history around their necks.

title: How every Paris 2024 Olympic medal will contain pieces of the Eiffel Tower
author: Flo Clifford
description: This year the victors will earn a unique reward - a piece of French history
